text: This video presents the case of a surgical procedure performed in a sixteen years old girl presenting with a huge mass in the tail of pancreas. The patient complained of abdominal pain. The CT scan demonstrated a ten centimeter lesion including both solid and cystic areas and calcification. Tumor markers were normal. Preoperative diagnosis was solid.
or vein was not observed. Considering the radiologic findings, a spleen-preserving distal pyogradectomy is decided.
The surgeon exposes the tumor located behind the greater omentum, with the gastrocolic ligament visibly divided to access the surgical site.
The surgeon divides adhesions between the posterior gastric wall and the pancreas, separating these structures to improve access to the surgical site.
The surgeon divides the inferior and posterior attachments of the pancreas, utilizing hue coloring to differentiate tissue planes during the dissection.
The surgeon exposes the splenic vein, revealing its location and orientation in relation to surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon isolates the splenic artery and vein, clearly separating these vascular structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon transects the pancreas with a tumor-free margin using a 60mm green cartridge and GIS stapler. Subsequently, veins between the pancreas and the splenic vein are isolated and divided.
The specimen is placed into an endo bag and extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision. A Jackson-Pratt drain is then positioned in the surgical site.
